questions.text: I hold the view that the weight I give to others' pain in my moral reasoning is the most transparent indicator of my real priorities.
I find that I am most helpful in emotionally charged environments when I can maintain my composure and offer a calm, structured perspective.
I tend to let the group define its own boundaries collectively.
I find that my emotional steadiness during periods of others' distress is a contribution I can sustain without depleting myself.
I tend to keep a running tally of my personal wins and review them when I need motivation.
I tend to treat all conversations as primarily about exchanging information.
I would endure personal hardship rather than let someone I know struggle alone.
I find that skipping the planning phase allows me to be more present during execution.
When I feel truly heard and understood, the relief is so profound it can make me cry.
I maintain detailed records and notes for projects I'm involved in.
I hold the view that time spent perfecting my craft is never wasted.
I feel that if no one leads, nothing meaningful gets accomplished.
I plan my meals, workouts, or daily routines in advance.
I prefer to explore possibilities as they arise rather than commit to a single long-term plan.
I pick up on when a person's compliment is actually a way of drawing attention to something they disapprove of.
I believe that the moral importance of suffering is not a variable that changes with my mood — it is a constant that I orient my decisions around.
I find it difficult to tell whether a group's silence indicates contentment or suppressed disagreement.
I rarely notice when a person's posture closes off in response to something I said.
I feel that the moral significance I assign to others' suffering is an active choice that I renew each time I encounter it.
I feel responsible for making sure everyone is part of what happens.
I notice that even anticipating a difficult conversation makes my palms sweat and my heart race.
I feel that I should always leave a situation better for the next person.
I detect when a group is performing harmony to avoid conflict.
I'd rather enjoy what's available now than wait for something potentially better later.
I experience the space between stimulus and response as comfortably wide.
I notice that I can witness intense emotion in others and feel present for them without absorbing it into my own body.
My eyes water not just from sadness but from any strong emotion — rage, joy, awe, gratitude.
I hold the view that personal benchmarks—income, fitness, skills—are the truest measures of progress.
I think that remaining detached during emotionally intense situations is a form of professional discipline I value highly.
I usually frame debates by stating my position clearly at the start.
I feel that the moment I stop giving is the moment I stop living fully.
I experience embarrassment as a passing moment rather than a lingering burn.
I find that I register the emotional weight of a pause in conversation.
I feel most capable when I have unilateral authority over a domain.
I make decisions by weighing the long-term consequences carefully before acting.
I am known for bridging differences in group deliberations.
